chunks.header: Written evidence submitted by UK Finance (Part 1/2)
chunks.text: UK Finance members welcome the Public Accounts Committee Call for Evidence on the preparedness of the online safety regulation. We believe examining the implementation detail will leadtoamore efficient and proportionate framework, which in turn helps to accelerate and enhance effective online safety controls. More specifically, UK Finance members note:  Over £1.2 billion was stolen through fraud in 2022, with nearly 80 per cent of Authorised Push Payment (APP) scam cases starting online and 3rd Party fraud also significantly impacted. Whist the online bill looks to ensure customers are safe online, there are illegal harms that originate from data compromise and harvesting online that enables 3 rd party fraud in addition to APP Scams. The distinction of these two are as would be paramount to ensuring future data sharing will support both the protecting the customer from ‘real time’ APP scams (£485.2mn), and in the future from 3 rd party access by criminals which leads to unauthorised fraud (£726.9mn).  Given the importance of the role of Ofcom as the Online Safety Regulator, it is important Ofcom is a dequately resourced, funded, and prepared (including skill and expertise) for the enormous taskthatlays ahead of them  As Ofcom’s in for mation gathering powers donotcomeinto place until Royal Assent of the Online Safety Bill (OSB), the regulator will have produce codes of practice for consultation without having had the benefit and insight that these powers can provide. As a result, there is a risk there will be an iterative approach with the codes of practice, and this will create extended lead times for effective implementation. We believe that the in for mation gathering powers will need to be leveraged during the earliest stages of the consultation cycle to ensure the most robust codes of practice can be created in the first instance.  The precision of Ofcom’s codes of conduct and risk assessment guidance will determine the efficacy of the online safety regulation and implementation by theplatforms. As such, these codes and risk assessments should have a focus on impact and incidents as a core consideration , rather than the relative volume of victims as the impact faced could be significant. Proportionate controls based on legitimate and illegitimate activity could be overshadowed the harms faced by victims given the size of the online platforms.  There is an urgent need for supporting mechanisms to ensure delivery against the ‘duty to operate a service using proportionate systems and processes designed to— (a) minimise the length of time for which any priority illegal content is present; (b) where the provider is a lerted by a person to the presence of any illegal content, or becomes aware of it in any other way, swiftly takedownsuch content.’ o Theplatforms typically provide either Consumer or Law en for cement reporting options which either dilutes orsetsthebar too high for the regulated sector intelligence.

We believe examining the implementation detail will leadtoamore efficient and proportionate framework, which in turn helps to accelerate and enhance effective online safety controls. As a result, there is a risk there will be an iterative approach with the codes of practice, and this will create extended lead times for effective implementation. As such, these codes and risk assessments should have a focus on impact and incidents as a core consideration , rather than the relative volume of victims as the impact faced could be significant. Proportionate controls based on legitimate and illegitimate activity could be overshadowed the harms faced by victims given the size of the online platforms.  There is an urgent need for supporting mechanisms to ensure delivery against the ‘duty to operate a service using proportionate systems and processes designed to— (a) minimise the length of time for which any priority illegal content is present; (b) where the provider is a lerted by a person to the presence of any illegal content, or becomes aware of it in any other way, swiftly takedownsuch content.’ o Theplatforms typically provide either Consumer or Law en for cement reporting options which either dilutes orsetsthebar too high for the regulated sector intelligence.

chunks.header: Written evidence submitted by UK Finance (Part 2/2)
chunks.text: provider is a lerted by a person to the presence of any illegal content, or becomes aware of it in any other way, swiftly takedownsuch content.’ o Theplatforms typically provide either Consumer or Law en for cement reporting options which either dilutes orsetsthebar too high for the regulated sector intelligence. This significantly limits the rich intelligence available from the regulated sectors impacted by online harms, as the FS sector often has actionable intelligence, that can often be explicitly linked to criminality. However, currently manyplatforms have ‘conduct rules’ and therefore will not remove content that is not explicitly illegal unless it breaches these conduct rules. Definition and alignment of the key requirements for removal of harmful content should be defined before data sharing to ensure consistency and efficiency of action offtheback of data sharing between platforms and financial services. o There is also currently no mechanism available to feed this in for mation in to online platforms from regulated sectors, nor any SLAs for treatment of the issues identified. Please see Appendix A for an example of a new online harm that continued unmitigated for multiple weeks, this resulted in tens of thousands of victims as well as criminals attempting to defraud over £3mn across the Financial Services sector.  The OSB focuses on content moderation and does not clearly create obligations around business models that introduce risks to consumers. The online marketplace available on social media has become the most prominent driver of scams in the UK, almost 80% of purchase scams are sourced from one marketplace alone. Other marketplaces implement payment and ecosystem controls to instil customer trust, and this become a self-fulfilling model incentivising theplatforms to maintain safe environments, as any rogue or illigitimate activity would be a mplified due to the trust embedded with the user by that platform. It is imperative the risk assessments guidance provided by Ofcom also assesses the risks of the business models introduce that materialise online harms. We also look forward to further engagement with Ofcom as it considers the implementation of the regulation that operationalises the new powers the Online Safety Bill will bring. We thank you in advance for your consideration and we remain at your disposal should you wish to discuss any component of our response. October 2023

chunks.text: In a five-week period just one member bankhadmorethan 3000 victims and £600k attempted fraud as a result of these adverts appearing on a platform. Multiple firms were impacted by this, and across the industry the impact is estimated to have resulted in 20k victims and circa £3.5mn of attempted fraud. The scale of attack and impacts are significant for the operational teams of the banks, even from a single piece of content on an online platform. Below are three examples of the contents victims would see int this attack across August – September 2023. Example 1 – WILKO Link to fraudulent webpage shared with UKF – now removed. • Customer presented with fake WILKO Sale advert on Facebook. • Customer presented with fake WILKO Sale advert on Facebook. • Advert leads to a Facebook page containing a link to the malicious website. • Website displays items at heavily discounted rate. ay enrolment. • Customer attempts to make a purchase and proceeds to the payment screen disclosing necessary ianyfeonrmroalmtioenntf. or Apple /Google P • Customer receives Apple/Google Pay activation OTP and discloses intowebpage under the impression it is a 3DS step up. • Fraudulently provisioned card used by fraudster. • No transaction is seen to WILKO. • Fraudulent token spend undertaken at Zettle.
